items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to Berlin' (by train) in German?
items.why: Students use gehen for any 'going'. In German, gehen = go ON FOOT only. FAHREN = go by vehicle (car, train, bus). FLIEGEN = go by plane. To Berlin by train: ich fahre. Saying 'ich gehe nach Berlin' suggests walking there!

items.q: How do you say 'I'm travelling to Germany' in German?
items.why: Students use 'in' or 'zu'. For most countries and cities: NACH (Deutschland, Berlin, Paris). For countries with articles (die Schweiz, die USA): IN with accusative. Easy to mix up - nach is for proper place names without articles.

items.q: What case does 'helfen' (to help) require for its object?
items.why: Students assume direct objects are always accusative. Certain German verbs (helfen, danken, gratulieren, folgen, antworten) take DATIVE objects despite appearing transitive in English.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I am going by train to Berlin'?
items.correct: Ich fahre mit dem Zug nach Berlin.
items.misconception: Ich fahre mit der Zug nach Berlin.
items.random_wrong: Ich fahre auf dem Zug nach Berlin.
items.why: It's Ich fahre mit dem Zug nach Berlin.. “Ich fahre mit der Zug nach Berlin.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which case do prepositions like 'mit' and 'zu' always take?
items.why: Correct answer: dative. “accusative” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many German-speaking countries are there in the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 countries. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
